items.mistake: Setting max_features=1.0 so every split sees all features
items.fix: Use sqrt(p) for classification or a fraction (e.g. 0.33) for regression unless you have a reason to disable decorrelation.
items.why: Without feature subsampling, bagged trees stay correlated when one dominant feature wins every split, weakening variance reduction.
items.example: On 50 features with one strong predictor, max_features='sqrt' forces trees to sometimes split on weaker features, improving ensemble diversity.

items.mistake: Expecting more trees to overfit like boosting rounds
items.fix: Increasing n_estimators usually lowers variance (up to a plateau); tune depth and max_features if validation error is high, not tree count alone.
items.why: Forest trees are averaged, not added with full weight; extra trees add independent votes rather than chasing training residuals.
items.example: Moving from 100 to 500 trees often smooths OOB error downward; validation may plateau but rarely spikes solely from more trees.

items.mistake: Treating random forest like gradient boosting (sequential, residual-based)
items.fix: Train all trees in parallel on independent bootstraps; tune max_features and depth, not learning_rate or pseudo-residuals.
items.why: Confusing the two ensemble types leads to wrong hyperparameter choices and incorrect expectations about early stopping on residuals.
items.example: There is no F ← F + η·h_m update—final class is majority vote across trees trained on different bootstrap draws.
